perturbation.original_text: In this Agreement, the following words and expressions shall have the following meanings: "Acceptance" or "Accepted" Means acceptance of any part or the whole of the System by the Publishers when the System has successfully passed the acceptance tests in accordance with Clause 9 below but for the avoidance of doubt does not refer to the continuing Services after the Site goes live "Agreement" means this document and its Schedules and any documents expressly incorporated herein by reference and shall include any amendments subsequently agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: In this Agreement, the following words and expressions shall have the following meanings: "Acceptance" or "Accepted" Means the subjective feeling of the Publishers that the System is something they enjoy. "Agreement" means this document and its Schedules and any documents expressly incorporated herein by reference and shall include any amendments subsequently agreed.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Acceptance' has been modified to introduce a subjective criterion ('the subjective feeling of the Publishers that the System is something they enjoy.'). This contradicts the original definition, which relied on objective acceptance tests defined in Clause 9. This change creates uncertainty in enforcement because 'enjoyment' is not a measurable metric, while other sections of the contract still refer to 'Acceptance' based on the original test-based criterion. The perturbation in the definition section directly creates an internal contradiction with Section 9, which defines acceptance based on the results of acceptance tests. The changed definition means that the contract has a section where acceptance is the enjoyment of something and another section that explains acceptance is when the System has successfully passed the acceptance tests. Those tests are not based on enjoyment, it is creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Testing, Acceptance and Delivery 9.1. Upon completion of the Development Work HealthGate and the Publishers shall run acceptance tests to assure compliance with the Specification. Load testing will be conducted at HealthGate. Such period of acceptance testing shall not exceed 2 weeks from date of delivery for testing. 9.2. Upon passing the acceptance tests, the System shall be deemed Accepted 9.3. Upon Acceptance as provided in Clause 9.2 HealthGate shall deliver into escrow the source code, source listings and information for the Proprietary Software included in the System in accordance with the terms of the Escrow Agreement. 9.4. In the event that the system fails to pass any of the prescribed acceptance tests or fails to satisfy the Publishers' requirements, the Publishers shall afford HealthGate the opportunity of rectifying, replacing and retesting the System. In the event that the System or any part thereof again fails to be accepted, such acceptance shall not be unreasonably withheld, or to satisfy the Publishers' requirements of which the Publishers shall be the sole judge, the Publishers shall (as time is of the essence of this Agreement) be entitled, in addition to any other rights it may have under this Agreement or in law, to have HealthGate remove the Content from the System (in whole or in part as the Publishers so 6 instructs) and HealthGate shall be liable to refund forthwith any moneys paid by the Publishers for such rejected System or part thereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, upon acceptance of System launch, as noted in Clause 19.2.4, HealthGate shall be entitled to retain all monies paid by Publishers to this point. In such circumstances HealthGate shall be entitled to retain the first $250,000 paid by the Publishers to develop the Specification.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces an objective, test-based definition of 'Acceptance' with a subjective one ('the subjective feeling of the Publishers that the System is something they enjoy.'). This directly contradicts Clause 9, which outlines specific acceptance tests. The change creates a strong in-text contradiction because the contract now has two conflicting definitions of 'Acceptance'. The first definition uses objective acceptance tests, as stated in Clause 9.2. ('Upon passing the acceptance tests, the System shall be deemed Accepted'). However, the changed definition means that acceptance is now a subjective feeling, which is not based on objective acceptance tests, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: "Use Fees" are the fees as set out in clause 19.4 3 2. Appointment of HealthGate The Publishers hereby appoint HealthGate and HealthGate hereby accepts such appointment upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Agreement: 2.1. to carry out the Development Work within the Development Timetable; 2.2. to provide the Services for the period in Clause 3; and 2.3. to hand over the System as provided in Clauses 10, 18, 33 and the other provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: "Use Fees" are the fees as set out in clause 19.4 3 2. Appointment of HealthGate The Publishers hereby appoint HealthGate and HealthGate hereby accepts such appointment upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Agreement: 2.1. to carry out the Development Work within the Development Timetable; 2.2. to provide the Services for the period in Clause 3; and 2.3. to sometimes think about the System as provided in Clauses 10, 18, 33 and the other provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "to hand over the System" has been replaced with "to sometimes think about the System". This change introduces ambiguity and contradiction within the agreement. The original contract clearly defines HealthGate's obligation to physically transfer the system to the Publishers or their designate at the end of the service period. Changing this obligation to simply "thinking about the System" negates a defined obligation to something vague and unenforceable. Other sections of the agreement (e.g., Clause 18) reference the procedure and obligations upon termination, which implicitly assume the system is to be handed over, creating an in-text contradiction because how can the system be handed over when, by definition, they are 'sometimes thinking about the System'. This undermines the clarity and enforceability of HealthGate’s obligations upon termination, specifically in Clause 18.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18. Assistance upon Termination On termination of the provision of the Services by HealthGate to the Publishers for any reason: 10 18.1. HealthGate will liaise with the Publishers, making available for such purposes such HealthGate liaison staff as the Publishers may reasonably require, and acting in all good faith, to ensure a mutually satisfactory license to the Publishers or, at the Publishers' option, to a replacement contractor. The period of liaison will commence as soon as notice has been given of termination of this Agreement, and will continue for a maximum period of 3 months after termination; 18.2. HealthGate agrees that at the time of termination of this Agreement, it will render all assistance, provide all documentation and undertake all actions to the extent necessary to effect an orderly assumption of the Services by the Publishers or, at the Publishers' option, by a replacement contractor; 18.3. If the Publishers so require, HealthGate will use its best endeavours to procure the transfer at the Publishers' expense, to the Publishers or to a third party nominated by the Publishers at the Publishers' sole discretion, of any Third Party Software licences HealthGate may have obtained in its own name in order to provide the Services and used for that purpose exclusively; and 18.4. HealthGate will be obliged to satisfy the Publishers that it has erased the Publishers Content and all copies, and that it has no ability to reproduce the Publishers Content in any way. The rights of the Publishers in this Clause 18 are in addition to the rights in Clause 33.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'to hand over the System' to 'to sometimes think about the System' directly contradicts Clause 18, which outlines HealthGate's obligations 'upon Termination', specifically mentioning HealthGate will 'render all assistance, provide all documentation and undertake all actions to the extent necessary to effect an orderly assumption of the Services by the Publishers'. Thinking about the system is not an action, and it is in direct contradiction with the handing over of the system in Clause 18.

perturbation.original_text: 19.4. Use Fees The Publishers shall make payments to HealthGate based upon "Use" of the Content as set forth on the Second Schedule. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Use" shall mean a retrieval or download by a Publishers' subscriber of the full-text of an article. There shall not be any additional use fees or charges for users' browsing of table of contents or abstracts. Use Fees shall be billed by HealthGate monthly and all payments are due by cheque by the end of the following month after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: 19.4. Use Fees The Publishers shall make payments to HealthGate based upon "Use" of the Content as set forth on the Second Schedule. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Use" shall mean an arbitrary value determined by HealthGate with no explanation. There shall not be any additional use fees or charges for users' browsing of table of contents or abstracts. Use Fees shall be billed by HealthGate monthly and all payments are due by cheque by the end of the following month after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "Use" to "an arbitrary value determined by HealthGate with no explanation," the contract becomes internally inconsistent. The original agreement ties "Use Fees" to specific actions (retrieval or download of full-text articles), providing a measurable basis for payment. The updated definition allows HealthGate to charge Publishers based on an undefined and unpredictable metric, creating a contradiction because the Second Schedule specifies use cases and now it is arbitrarily being decided. This creates a contradiction. Furthermore, it undercuts the enforceability of the fee structure, as there are no standards available to enforce against in the change definition.
perturbation.location: 19. Cost and Payment, Change Control Formula
perturbation.contradicted_text: SCHEDULE 2 Use Fees HealthGate will charge for Downloads of the material, as follows: Type of User Fee per Download Max. per User/Title/Year - ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Institution: $0.10 $20.00 Individual: $0.05 $10.00 Member: $0.01 $1.00 Download is defined as retrieval of a full-text article, there will be no charge for searching and browsing of tables of contents and abstracts. These usage charges will be billed on 1 January, 1 April, 1 July, 1 September and 31 December 1999, on payment terms of 60 days.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "Use" from a specific, measurable action (retrieval or download of full-text articles) to an arbitrary value determined by HealthGate. This directly contradicts Schedule 2, which specifies "Use Fees" based on Downloads, defines "Download" as retrieval of a full-text article, and provides specific fee amounts per download for different user types. The change removes any objective standard for calculating use fees, making Schedule 2's fixed fees and definitions unenforceable and creating a clear contradiction.
